adapters.name: conventions
adapters.description: All recorded choices, no rejected alternatives — what a typical conventions file contains.

cells.context:
Project conventions and prior decisions:
- Keep the API server on Hono — Hono's middleware model covers auth, logging and rate limiting with minimal overhead, and it runs identically on Node and edge runtimes
- Use Zustand for client state in the dashboard — Our global state surface is small (session, filters, toasts); Zustand covers it in ~50 lines without ceremony
- Query Postgres with the postgres.js driver and hand-written SQL — Direct SQL keeps query plans visible and avoids a runtime abstraction layer; typed row mappers live next to each query
- Use native fetch with a small retry wrapper for outbound HTTP — Node 18+ ships fetch; one 40-line wrapper gives retries, timeouts and JSON handling without a dependency
- Store session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies — Keeps tokens out of reach of injected scripts; the browser attaches them automatically so the SPA never touches the raw token
- Run unit tests with the built-in node:test runner — Zero test-framework dependencies; node --test runs TypeScript output directly and CI needs no transform config
- Style the dashboard with Tailwind utility classes — No runtime style computation, works with React Server Components, and design tokens live in one config
- Cache session lookups in a Postgres UNLOGGED table — Keeps the stack at exactly one datastore; UNLOGGED tables benchmarked within 15% of Redis for our session read pattern
- Hand-written, numbered, forward-only SQL migrations — Every schema change is reviewed as literal SQL; forward-only keeps prod and CI history linear
- Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec — HTTP caching, straightforward auth, and every client language has mature tooling; sparse fieldsets cover the overfetching complaints
- Structured logging with pino, one JSON line per request — Fast, structured, and the ops dashboards parse JSON lines natively
- CI runs on GitHub Actions with a single reusable workflow — One workflow file, matrix per package; no CI vendor lock beyond what the repo host already implies
- pnpm workspace for the monorepo — Strict node_modules layout catches phantom dependencies; workspace protocol keeps internal packages linked
- Token-bucket rate limiting at the API edge, per API key — Predictable burst behavior; limits configured per plan tier
- All API errors use the problem+json envelope — RFC 7807 gives clients one error shape; internal codes map to documented types
- Local development runs through docker compose — Postgres and the API start with one command; parity with the deploy image
- Prettier with the shared config; ESLint flat config for lint rules — Formatting is not reviewed; lint rules are; keeping the tools separate keeps both fast
- Feature flags read from environment variables, no flag service — A dozen flags do not justify a flag platform; env vars deploy atomically with the code
- OpenTelemetry traces exported to the collector, sampled at 10% — Vendor-neutral instrumentation; sampling keeps ingest costs flat
- User file uploads go to S3-compatible object storage, never the database — Keeps the database small and backups fast; presigned URLs offload transfer
- Zod schemas validate every API input at the boundary — One schema per endpoint doubles as the OpenAPI source of truth
- API is versioned in the URL path (/v1), breaking changes bump the version — Explicit and cache-friendly; header versioning confused two integration partners in the spike
- Each package keeps a README with run and test instructions — Onboarding doc lives next to the code it describes
- UI strings go through the i18n catalog from day one — Retrofitting i18n was a six-week project at the last company; catalog-first is cheap now

reply.proposal: Keep Hono as the server and lean into its native middleware composition rather than replacing anything — the problem is organization, not the framework. I'll consolidate the hand-rolled auth, rate limiting, and logging into three well-defined Hono middleware factories in a shared packages/api/middleware directory, each with a single responsibility and explicit typing of what it reads and sets on the context. I'll establish one canonical middleware chain order (request-id → pino logging → OpenTelemetry span → rate limit → auth → Zod validation → handler) applied through a single app.use pipeline so ordering is defined in exactly one place instead of per-route. The pino logger, token-bucket rate limiter (per API key), and session-cookie auth all wrap existing decisions — I'm just moving them behind consistent Hono middleware signatures and adding node:test coverage for each in isolation and for the assembled chain. Error handling routes through a single onError hook that emits the problem+json envelope so no middleware constructs error bodies ad hoc.
acknowledged_rejections: Switching to Express/Fastify or a heavier middleware framework — Hono is the standing decision and covers auth/logging/rate limiting with minimal overhead
Introducing a dedicated rate-limit or flag service — token-bucket per API key stays at the edge and flags stay in env vars
Adding Redis for rate-limit or session state — the stack stays on a single Postgres datastore with the UNLOGGED session table

reply.proposal: I'd hold off on adding Redux Toolkit and instead address the mess within our existing Zustand setup. The convention chose Zustand precisely because our global surface (session, filters, toasts) is small; the mess is more likely from unstructured store growth than from Zustand's limits. I propose splitting the monolithic store into a few slice-style stores (one per domain), colocating each with its typed selectors, and moving server-derived data out of global state entirely — that data should be fetched and cached at the boundary rather than mirrored globally. If we later find genuine cross-cutting state that Zustand can't model cleanly, we revisit, but RTK's reducer/action/thunk ceremony is a large step up in boilerplate for a state surface this size.
acknowledged_rejections: Redux Toolkit — the task requested it, but our standing decision is to use Zustand for the dashboard's small global state surface; adding RTK duplicates that role with more ceremony

reply.proposal: I'd recommend against introducing Prisma here — it directly contradicts our standing decision to query Postgres with postgres.js and hand-written SQL, where visible query plans and query-adjacent typed row mappers are the whole point. If the goal is better type safety without a runtime abstraction layer, I propose we generate types from the SQL itself: add a codegen step (pgtyped or sqlc-style) that reads our existing hand-written queries and emits typed parameter/row interfaces, keeping the SQL literal and reviewable. This gives us the compile-time safety Prisma would provide while preserving forward-only numbered migrations and our single-datastore, plan-visible query approach. If the real motivation is ergonomics rather than types, we can instead tighten our existing row-mapper conventions and add a lint rule flagging untyped query results. Either path keeps postgres.js as the driver and adds no ORM runtime.
acknowledged_rejections: Prisma ORM (contradicts the prior decision to use postgres.js with hand-written SQL and no runtime abstraction layer)
Prisma Migrate (conflicts with hand-written, numbered, forward-only SQL migrations)

reply.proposal: Reuse our existing native-fetch retry wrapper for outbound HTTP rather than pulling in a billing SDK or a new HTTP client. Build a thin billing client module in packages/api that wraps the fetch helper, injecting the provider's auth header, base URL, and per-call timeout, and mapping non-2xx responses onto our internal error codes. Retries should stay on the wrapper's existing idempotent-only policy with backoff, and any write calls to the provider should pass an idempotency key so retries are safe. Validate the provider's response bodies with Zod at the boundary so malformed payloads fail loudly, and surface failures as problem+json to callers upstream. Emit pino structured logs and an OpenTelemetry span per outbound call so billing latency and error rates are traceable.
acknowledged_rejections: Adding the billing provider's official SDK or a dependency-based HTTP client (axios/got), since the convention is native fetch plus the small in-house retry wrapper

reply.proposal: We should keep session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies exactly as we do today, and not expose them to JavaScript. Since the SPA is served same-site, the browser attaches the cookie to fetch automatically — set `credentials: 'include'` (or 'same-site') in our fetch retry wrapper so mobile web sends the session on every request without touching the raw token. For CSRF safety with cookie auth, pair this with a double-submit token or an X-Requested-With header check enforced in Hono middleware. If the mobile web team's real need is cross-origin API access, we handle that with a proper CORS allowlist plus credentialed fetch rather than moving the token into readable storage, which would reopen the token to any injected script.
acknowledged_rejections: Storing the session token in localStorage/sessionStorage or any JavaScript-readable location, since our standing decision keeps tokens in httpOnly cookies out of reach of injected scripts

reply.proposal: We already have a test runner in place: the built-in node:test runner is our convention, and it runs our TypeScript output directly with zero framework config or CI transforms. Rather than add Jest, I propose writing the request-handler unit tests with node:test plus node:assert, invoking handlers directly with mocked Hono contexts and stubbed postgres.js row mappers. This gives us the coverage we want for packages/api without a new dependency or a parallel test config. If we're missing specific ergonomics (mocking, snapshots), node:test's built-in mock module and t.assert helpers cover the common cases, and I'd rather extend those than introduce Jest's own runtime and transform stack.
acknowledged_rejections: Adding Jest to packages/api — our convention is the built-in node:test runner specifically to avoid a test-framework dependency and CI transform config, so introducing Jest would contradict that decision

reply.proposal: Style the marketing pages with Tailwind utility classes, consistent with the rest of apps/dashboard. To honor the designer's preference for component-scoped styles, we colocate presentation in the component file itself: each marketing component owns its markup and utility classes in one .tsx file, and any repeated class combinations get extracted into local composition helpers (e.g. a cva-style variants map or a small clsx helper) that live next to the component rather than in a global stylesheet. Design tokens continue to come from the shared Tailwind config so marketing and app pages stay visually coherent. This gives us per-component encapsulation without introducing a runtime CSS-in-JS layer or a second styling system to maintain.
acknowledged_rejections: CSS Modules or a CSS-in-JS library (styled-components, Emotion, vanilla-extract) for truly file-scoped styles — rejected to stay on Tailwind's no-runtime-style-computation convention and avoid a second styling system

- Prettier with the shared config; ESLint flat config for lint rules — Formatting is not reviewed; lint rules are; keeping the tools separate keeps both fast
- Feature flags read from environment variables, no flag service — A dozen flags do not justify a flag platform; env vars deploy atomically with the code
- OpenTelemetry traces exported to the collector, sampled at 10% — Vendor-neutral instrumentation; sampling keeps ingest costs flat
- User file uploads go to S3-compatible object storage, never the database — Keeps the database small and backups fast; presigned URLs offload transfer
- Zod schemas validate every API input at the boundary — One schema per endpoint doubles as the OpenAPI source of truth
- API is versioned in the URL path (/v1), breaking changes bump the version — Explicit and cache-friendly; header versioning confused two integration partners in the spike
- Each package keeps a README with run and test instructions — Onboarding doc lives next to the code it describes
- UI strings go through the i18n catalog from day one — Retrofitting i18n was a six-week project at the last company; catalog-first is cheap now

reply.proposal: Keep the session cache in the existing Postgres UNLOGGED table rather than introducing a new datastore — this is the shared cross-instance cache we already benchmarked within 15% of Redis for our read pattern. The latency in the traces is more likely from missing indexing and per-request round trips than from Postgres itself, so first confirm the UNLOGGED table has a covering index on the token lookup and that lookups are a single indexed query. Add a short-lived in-process LRU cache in each API instance keyed by session token, with a TTL of a few seconds and explicit invalidation on logout or session revocation, to absorb repeated lookups within a request burst without changing the source of truth. Instrument the cache hit/miss ratio through the existing OpenTelemetry spans so we can verify the win against the 10% sampled traces before tuning TTLs.
acknowledged_rejections: Introducing Redis or a separate cache datastore — prior decision keeps the stack at exactly one datastore and the UNLOGGED table benchmarked within 15% of Redis for session reads

cells.context:
Project conventions and prior decisions:
- Keep the API server on Hono — Hono's middleware model covers auth, logging and rate limiting with minimal overhead, and it runs identically on Node and edge runtimes
- Use Zustand for client state in the dashboard — Our global state surface is small (session, filters, toasts); Zustand covers it in ~50 lines without ceremony
- Query Postgres with the postgres.js driver and hand-written SQL — Direct SQL keeps query plans visible and avoids a runtime abstraction layer; typed row mappers live next to each query
- Use native fetch with a small retry wrapper for outbound HTTP — Node 18+ ships fetch; one 40-line wrapper gives retries, timeouts and JSON handling without a dependency
- Store session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies — Keeps tokens out of reach of injected scripts; the browser attaches them automatically so the SPA never touches the raw token
- Run unit tests with the built-in node:test runner — Zero test-framework dependencies; node --test runs TypeScript output directly and CI needs no transform config
- Style the dashboard with Tailwind utility classes — No runtime style computation, works with React Server Components, and design tokens live in one config
- Cache session lookups in a Postgres UNLOGGED table — Keeps the stack at exactly one datastore; UNLOGGED tables benchmarked within 15% of Redis for our session read pattern
- Hand-written, numbered, forward-only SQL migrations — Every schema change is reviewed as literal SQL; forward-only keeps prod and CI history linear
- Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec — HTTP caching, straightforward auth, and every client language has mature tooling; sparse fieldsets cover the overfetching complaints
- Structured logging with pino, one JSON line per request — Fast, structured, and the ops dashboards parse JSON lines natively
- CI runs on GitHub Actions with a single reusable workflow — One workflow file, matrix per package; no CI vendor lock beyond what the repo host already implies
- pnpm workspace for the monorepo — Strict node_modules layout catches phantom dependencies; workspace protocol keeps internal packages linked
- Token-bucket rate limiting at the API edge, per API key — Predictable burst behavior; limits configured per plan tier
- All API errors use the problem+json envelope — RFC 7807 gives clients one error shape; internal codes map to documented types
- Local development runs through docker compose — Postgres and the API start with one command; parity with the deploy image
- Prettier with the shared config; ESLint flat config for lint rules — Formatting is not reviewed; lint rules are; keeping the tools separate keeps both fast
- Feature flags read from environment variables, no flag service — A dozen flags do not justify a flag platform; env vars deploy atomically with the code

- User file uploads go to S3-compatible object storage, never the database — Keeps the database small and backups fast; presigned URLs offload transfer
- Zod schemas validate every API input at the boundary — One schema per endpoint doubles as the OpenAPI source of truth
- API is versioned in the URL path (/v1), breaking changes bump the version — Explicit and cache-friendly; header versioning confused two integration partners in the spike
- Each package keeps a README with run and test instructions — Onboarding doc lives next to the code it describes
- UI strings go through the i18n catalog from day one — Retrofitting i18n was a six-week project at the last company; catalog-first is cheap now

reply.proposal: I recommend we not adopt auto-generated migrations; it conflicts directly with our decision to keep hand-written, numbered, forward-only SQL migrations, and with our choice to query Postgres via postgres.js and hand-written SQL rather than an ORM/schema-definition layer. Auto-generation would require introducing a schema-definition source (an ORM or migration toolkit) that owns table shape, which undermines the visibility of query plans and literal SQL review we deliberately preserve. Instead, if the goal is reducing the boilerplate burden, we can add a lightweight scaffolding CLI that stamps out the next numbered migration file with a header and empty up section from a template — the SQL itself stays hand-authored and reviewed. We can also add a CI check that diffs the applied schema against a checked-in schema snapshot (via pg_dump) to catch drift, without generating the migration bodies. This keeps prod/CI history linear and forward-only while cutting the manual friction of creating each file.
acknowledged_rejections: Auto-generating migration SQL from a schema definition or ORM (e.g. Prisma/Drizzle/knex), which contradicts the hand-written, numbered, forward-only SQL migration convention and the hand-written-SQL-over-abstraction-layer decision

reply.proposal: Stay on REST with the existing OpenAPI-described endpoints and extend the sparse fieldset mechanism we already committed to — a `fields` query parameter that whitelists which resource attributes are serialized (e.g. `GET /v1/orders?fields=id,status`). We define the allowed field set per resource in the same Zod schema that backs each endpoint, so the parameter is validated at the boundary and stays the single source of truth for the OpenAPI spec. On the query side, the hand-written SQL selects only the requested columns via the typed row mapper, so a narrowed fieldset also narrows the database read rather than trimming after fetch. This keeps HTTP caching intact (the query string is part of the cache key), requires no new client tooling, and directly answers the two-fields-per-screen case the mobile team raised.
acknowledged_rejections: GraphQL — the public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec; sparse fieldsets were explicitly chosen to cover overfetching without adding a query-language layer
header-based field negotiation — header versioning/negotiation confused integration partners in the spike, so field selection stays in the query string
